speaker: interviewee
text: I would say Poland, but I'm not sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: And would you know, more or less, when it was taken?

speaker: interviewer
text: Good. Shmuel, tell me about this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, when we arrived from Poland to Germany-- and this was the first few months-- we were living in a kibbutz, which is a collective. And-- and this place was just a collective place where all the survivors stayed. We existed together, and prepared ourselves to go to Israel. And this is perhaps at a gathering. I don't recollect when it was done. As a matter of fact, when I saw it, it was the first time in a long time that I realized I have got this picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the place?

speaker: interviewer
text: And this would be how long after the end of the war?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you in the picture?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you. Shmuel, tell me about this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- this is my [NON-ENGLISH]. This was taken at the beginning of first week in April 1950, in Haifa, Israel. This is a-- this a picture of when my [NON-ENGLISH] and I were married.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mua-- myself, with my daughter, Mirale. Is that right? Mirale. That's right. She does. She likes Mirale-- walking in Haifa, in the main street.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was our first trip overseas in Australia, 19-- in December 1972, and it was taken in Haifa.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who have we got here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Over here we got our two daughters-- Mirale and Debale.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you. Shmuel, tell me about these pictures.

speaker: interviewee
text: The one on the left is my elder daughter, Mirale. She is taken here while graduating in Sydney-- at Sydney Medical-- University of Sydney-- medicine, finishing medicine. And the one on the right is Debbie, graduating from the University of New South Wales, in cuma-- no-- arts law.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was Mira's picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1978, Mira says. Is that right, Mirale?

speaker: interviewee
text: We have got here our youngest grandson-- for the time being, the youngest, at least-- David, David Joshua. This is at the age of three, I would say, no? It's the age of three or four? Can you help me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Age of three. Taken in Connecticut, United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank-- OK, Shmuel, tell me about this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is a family picture taken in 1995. It is in my daughter's home. Is it? Yes. It's--

speaker: interviewee
text: Mirale's home, yes, Mirale's home. And to single out which one-- my son-in-law, Gary Grunstein, [POINTS] [NON-ENGLISH], should I say this, right? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Deborah, [POINTS] the one who ran away and lives now in the United States; Deborah's husband, Steven; [POINTING] David, their son; the old fellow, the dean; [NON-ENGLISH]; Jonathan Grunstein-- bar mitzvah-to-be; Adam Grunstein; and last, but not least, Mirale.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Shmuel, what occasion are we--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was a bar mitzvah in 1995-- Adam's bar mitzvah, Adam Grunstein's bar mitzvah, my eldest son-- the first bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. OK, Shmuel, tell me about this document.

speaker: interviewee
text: This document was issued after we came back from the exodus. It was issued by the Jewish authorities. You can see there was no name here, because at that time we were at odds with the British. And this was only document to show them this is who you are. We did not put our names. Even when they ask us, we didn't say our names. This were-- we were told so, and this is what it is. It was issued in 1948, I would say, by the secretariat of the exodus authorities. And they were taken away later. For whatever reasons, I don't know. But my wife, working in the office, has decided that she's entitled to be an exception, and took for me and her a document. I am very much in doubt if you will find any of these anywhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any idea why?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- it is too ago. It's nearly 50 years. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you. Shmuel, tell me about this document.

speaker: interviewee
text: This document was given-- handed to us-- each and every illegal migrants that arrived to Palestine, in 1947. It was handed out by the British authorities. And it explained to us in different languages-- it is in Hebrew, Jewish, German, and Czech, as far as I can see-- that we are not permitted to go in-- to land in Israel. And we will be taken to Cyprus, wait our queue, and then we'll be permitted to come in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever taken to Cyprus?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we were never taken to Cyprus. With this paper, we came back to Germany, after two months on the-- on the sea.
